title: Apply: human impacts on the environment
description: Apply your understanding of human impacts on the environment in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS
Apply: human impacts on the environment

assessment_items.question_content:
Meat is an important part of many people’s diets around the world. The amount of meat consumed—and therefore produced—globally has changed over time. The graph below shows how global meat production changed between 1975 and 2020.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statement.**

Between 1975 and 2020, global meat production approximately [[☃ dropdown 1]].

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Let’s study the graph to determine how global meat production changed between 1975 and 2020. First, let’s estimate global meat production in 1975:

[[☃ image 1]]

Approximately $\maroonD{115\; \text{million tonnes}}$ of meat were produced in 1975.

Then, let’s estimate global meat production in 2020:

[[☃ image 1]]

Approximately $\purpleD{340\; \text{million tonnes}}$ of meat were produced in 2020.

Finally, let’s compare the two values:

$\purpleD{340\; \text{million tonnes}} \approx \maroonD{115\; \text{million tonnes}} \times 3$
So, between 1975 and 2020, global meat production approximately *tripled*.

options.explanation: [Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)](https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#home)

options.alt: A line graph is shown. The horizontal axis is labeled Year and goes from 1975 to 2020. The vertical axis is labeled Global meat production (million tonnes) and goes from 0 to 400. The line starts at 1975 at roughly 115 million tonnes, increases steadily, and then ends at 2020 at roughly 340 million tonnes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Between 1975 and 2020, global meat production approximately tripled. One reason for this is an increase in the number of people in the world. The graph below shows how the world population changed between 1975 and 2020.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statements.**

Between 1975 and 2020, the world population approximately [[☃ dropdown 1]]. This [[☃ dropdown 2]] the increase in global meat production during this time.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Let’s study the graph to determine how the world population changed between 1975 and 2020. First, let’s estimate the world population in 1975:

[[☃ image 1]]

The world population was roughly $\maroonD{4.1\; \text{billion people}}$ in 1975.

Then, let’s estimate the world population in 2020:

[[☃ image 1]]

The world population was roughly $\purpleD{7.8\; \text{billion people}}$ in 2020.

Finally, let’s compare the two values:

$\purpleD{7.8\; \text{billion people}} \approx \maroonD{4.1\; \text{billion people}} \times 2$

So, between 1975 and 2020, the world population approximately *doubled*.

Now, let’s think about how well this explains the increase in global meat production. We know that global meat production tripled between 1975 and 2020. Since the world population only doubled during this time, it *does not fully explain* the increase in meat production.
So, between 1975 and 2020, the world population approximately doubled. This does not fully explain the increase in global meat production during this time.

options.alt: A line graph is shown. The horizontal axis is labeled Year and goes from 1975 to 2020. The vertical axis is labeled World population (billion people) and goes from 0 to 9. The line starts at 1975 at roughly 4.1 billion people, increases steadily, and then ends at 2020 at roughly 7.8 billion people.

assessment_items.question_content:
Global meat production depends on how many people there are in the world. But it also depends on how much meat each person eats. The amount of meat consumed by the average person has been increasing steadily over time. The table below gives the global meat consumption per person for two different years, 1975 and 2020.

Year | Global meat consumption per person ($\text{kg}$)
:-: | :-:
1975 | $28.4$
2020 | $43.2$

**By how many kilograms did global meat consumption per person increase between 1975 and 2020?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{kg}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: So, between 1975 and 2020, global meat consumption per person increased by $14.8\,\text{kg}$.

options.explanation: [Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)](https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#home). The closest available data point (2019) was used for 2020.

assessment_items.question_content:
Meat production helps feed a growing world population. However, it also has negative impacts on the environment. One of these impacts has to do with *land use*. In order to produce meat, land must be used to raise livestock and grow animal feed. Clearing land for these purposes can cause habitat loss, putting wild species at risk of extinction.

The following pie charts show the different ways that land is used on Earth today.

![Three pie charts are shown. The first pie chart is titled Earth’s land. It is divided into 2 parts labeled as follows: Habitable land 71%, Uninhabitable land 29%. The second pie chart is titled Habitable land. It is divided into 4 parts labeled as follows: Agricultural land 50%, Forests and shrub 48%, Lakes and rivers 1%, Urban land 1%. The third pie chart is titled Agricultural land. It is divided into 2 parts labeled as follows: Livestock and feed 77%, Crops 23%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18ca01235ecb23a3f1dae6ff1980126ed370f851)

**Complete the statements.**

According to the charts, [[☃ dropdown 1]] of Earth’s habitable land is used for agriculture. Of this agricultural land, most is used for [[☃ dropdown 2]].

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Let’s start by determining how much of Earth’s habitable land is used for agriculture. To do so, we need to study the second pie chart, which shows the breakdown of habitable land on Earth. In this chart, the slice representing agricultural land takes up 50% of the pie. So, *half* of Earth’s habitable land is used for agriculture.

Now, let’s determine what most of this agricultural land is used for. To do so, we need to study the third pie chart, which shows the breakdown of agricultural land on Earth. In this chart, the slice representing livestock and feed takes up the majority of the pie. So, most of the agricultural land on Earth is used for *livestock and feed*.
So, according to the charts, half of Earth’s habitable land is used for agriculture. Of this agricultural land, most is used for livestock and feed.

assessment_items.question_content:
As global meat production continues to rise, more and more of Earth’s land will be converted to agricultural land. As a result, the negative impacts of land use on wild species and their habitats will also increase.

In a recent study, a group of scientists looked at how an increase in agricultural land from 2010 to 2050 would affect different species. The study examined nearly $20{,}000$ amphibian, bird, and mammal species. For each species, the scientists predicted if it would gain, lose, or have no change in habitat due to new agricultural land. Their results are given in the table below.

Change in habitat by 2050 | Number of species
:-: | :-:
Habitat gain | $1{,}203$
Habitat loss | $17{,}409$
No change | $1{,}247$
**Total** | $19{,}859$

**Approximately what percent of the species are predicted to lose habitat by 2050?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest whole percent.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\%$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
We can determine what percent of the species are predicted to lose habitat by 2050 using the following equation:

$\text{\% of species predicted to lose habitat} = \dfrac{\maroonD{\text{\# of species predicted to lose habitat}}}{\purpleD{\text{total number of species}}} \times 100$

First, let’s find the numbers we need from the table:

Change in habitat by 2050 | Number of species
:-: | :-:
Habitat gain | $1{,}203$
Habitat loss | $\maroonD{17{,}409}$
No change | $1{,}247$
**Total** | $\purpleD{19{,}859}$

Then, let’s plug these numbers into our equation:

$\text{\% of species predicted to lose habitat} = \dfrac{\maroonD{17{,}409}}{\purpleD{19{,}859}} \times 100 \approx 87.66\%$

Rounding to the nearest whole percent, we get $88\%$.
So, $88\%$ of the species are predicted to lose habitat by 2050.

options.explanation: Williams, D. R., Clark, M., Buchanan, G. M., Ficetola, G. F., Rondinini, C. & Tilman, D. (2021). Proactive conservation to prevent habitat losses to agricultural expansion. *Nature Sustainability*, *4*, 314–322. [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41893-020-00656-5](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41893-020-00656-5)

assessment_items.question_content:
Agricultural land use has and will continue to cause habitat loss worldwide. One of the most impactful ways to reduce this type of land use is through diet changes. Research suggests that if the world moved to a plant-based diet, land use for agriculture would decrease by $75\%$. However, it isn’t practical or even ideal for everyone to stop eating meat and other animal products. Instead, many scientists propose reducing consumption of the products that use the most land.

The table below compares the land use of four different meats. The values represent the land needed to produce $100$ grams of protein, an essential nutrient found in meat and other foods.

Meat | Land use per $100$ grams of protein ($\text{m}^2$)
:-: | :-:
beef | $163.6$
chicken | $7.1$
lamb | $184.8$
pork | $10.7$

**Using the information in the table, label each bar on the bar graph.**

[[☃ label-image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Let’s start by labeling the top bar on the bar graph. This bar is the longest, so it should correspond to the *highest* value in the table.
The highest value in the table is $184.8\, \text{m}^2$, which belongs to lamb. So, the top bar should be labeled lamb.

Using the same approach, we can label the rest of the bars:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.imageAlt: A bar graph is shown. The vertical axis is labeled Meat. The horizontal axis is labeled Land use per 100 grams of protein (m superscript 2) and goes from 0 to 200. The vertical axis has, from top to bottom, 4 unlabeled bars. The first bar extends to roughly 185 and is the longest bar. The second bar extends to roughly 165 and is the second-longest bar. The third bar extends to roughly 10 and is the third-longest bar. The fourth bar extends to roughly 5 and is the shortest bar.

assessment_items.question_content:
Changing our diets can help reduce agricultural land use and its negative impacts on the environment. The graph below shows the land use per 100 grams of protein for four different meats. It also shows the land use for two different plant-based foods.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the graph, which two diet changes would likely reduce agricultural land use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph is shown. The horizontal axis is labeled Land use per 100 grams of protein (m superscript 2) and goes from 0 to 200. The vertical axis has, from top to bottom, 6 bars labeled Lamb, Beef, Pork, Chicken, Grains, and Tofu. The Lamb bar extends to 184.8. The Beef bar extends to 163.6. The Pork bar extends to 10.7. The Chicken bar extends to 7.1. The Grains bar extends to 4.6. The Tofu bar extends to 2.2.

choices.content: replacing beef with pork or chicken
replacing pork with lamb or beef
replacing any of the meats with grains or tofu
